market_context: Prediction markets indicate a bearish open for the S&P 500 on April 6 with only 42.5% probability of opening up, driven by EU tariff retaliation risks and thin weekend liquidity[1]. Friday's close showed mixed performance with S&P 500 at 6,582.69 (+0.11%), Dow at 46,504.67 (-0.13%), and Nasdaq at 21,879.18 (+0.18%), following stronger-than-expected March jobs data of 178,000 added and unemployment at 4.3%, which lifted Treasury yields to 4.35%[2]. March saw broad market declines of ~5% amid Middle East geopolitical risks, high inflation, and prior weak employment signals[3].

predictions.reasoning: Prediction markets show 42.5% chance of S&P 500 opening up amid EU tariff risks and thin liquidity, suggesting bearish sentiment[1]. Friday's S&P close at 6,582.69 follows March's 5% drop due to geopolitical tensions[2][3]. Elevated Treasury yields at 4.35% post-strong jobs data may pressure equities lower[2].

Dow closed Friday at 46,504.67 (-0.13%), extending March's ~5% decline amid Middle East risks and rising rates[2][3]. Tariff retaliation fears add downside pressure per prediction markets[1]. Strong jobs data lifted yields, weighing on rate-sensitive industrials[2].

Nasdaq closed Friday at 21,879.18 (+0.18%) but faces headwinds from March's 5% pullback and high inflation[2][3]. Bearish S&P open odds of 57.5% down imply tech sector vulnerability[1]. Higher yields post-178k jobs beat curb growth stock appeal[2].
